name: Cape Sada
access: 80 min drive from Matsuyama; monorail at cape ¥900
description: The westernmost point of Honshu — a bare volcanic cape reaching into the Bungo Channel between Ehime and Oita. A suspended monorail descends to the tip for 360° water views.

name: Dogo Onsen
access: Matsuyama City Tram, 20 min from JR Matsuyama station
description: Japan's oldest hot spring, continuously in use for 3,000 years. Rumored to be the model for the bathhouse in Spirited Away, it retains its magnificent Meiji-era wooden bathhouse building.

name: Ishite-ji Temple (Pilgrimage No.51)
access: 15 min walk from Dogo Onsen
description: The 51st of 88 sacred temples on the Shikoku pilgrimage — one of the most architecturally remarkable, with a corridor packed floor-to-ceiling with pilgrim offerings and a tunnel maze to walk.

name: Matsuyama Castle
access: Ropeway from central Matsuyama, 5 min
description: One of Japan's 12 original surviving castles, perched 132m above the city on a dramatic rocky summit. The ropeway delivers views of the Seto Inland Sea on clear days.

name: Omishima Island
access: Ferry from Imabari, 35 min; or cycling Shimanami Kaido
description: Home to Oyamazumi Shrine, the central shrine of all warrior deities — the greatest collection of ancient armor in Japan (9 national treasures) sits in a forest of 2,600-year-old camphor trees.

name: Ozu Castle
access: 15 min walk from Iyo-Ozu station (JR Yodo Line)
description: A fully reconstructed 2004 castle using only traditional Edo-era methods — hand-cut timber, no nails, no concrete — making it the most authentic modern castle reconstruction in Japan.

name: Shikoku Karst Plateau
access: 50 min drive from Matsuyama; no public transport to plateau
description: Japan's second-largest karst plateau straddles the Ehime-Kochi border at 1,400m — a sweep of white limestone outcrops, grazing dairy cows, and unobstructed Pacific views.

name: Shimanami Kaido Cycling (Ehime)
access: Start from Imabari station (JR Yosan Line, 40 min from Matsuyama)
description: The world's most beautiful cycling route — 70km across 10 islands on dedicated cycle lanes built into a 6-island bridge complex. Start from Imabari (Ehime) and finish in Onomichi (Hiroshima).

name: Uchiko Preserved Town
access: 15 min walk from Uchiko station (JR Yosan Line, 40 min from Matsuyama)
description: A beautifully preserved Edo-period merchant town that grew wealthy from wax production. The main street retains its original buildings and a stunning 19th-century kabuki theater still in use.

name: Uwajima Castle
access: 25 min walk from Uwajima station (JR Yodo Line)
description: A compact original castle on a hill overlooking Uwa Sea, famous for its association with bullfighting — the only city in Japan where bull-vs-bull sumo-style fights are a living tradition.

name: Chichibugahama Beach
access: 40 min drive from Takamatsu station; limited public transport
best_season: March–May, September–October (golden hour + low tide overlap)
description: Kagawa's 'Salar de Uyuni' — at low tide, a vast flat beach becomes a perfect mirror reflecting the sky, clouds and distant islands. The effect lasts only 30–60 minutes and requires specific conditions.

name: Kotohiragu Shrine (Konpira-san)
access: 15 min walk from Kotohira station (JR Dosan Line)
best_season: Year-round (avoiding peak summer heat for the climb)
description: Shikoku's most famous shrine, reached by climbing 1,368 steps up a forested mountain. The ascent takes 1–2 hours through stone lanterns, treasure halls, and increasingly wild forest.

name: Marugame Castle
access: 15 min walk from Marugame station (JR Yosan Line)
description: Japan's highest stone walls (60m fan-shaped ramparts), topped by one of Japan's 12 surviving original wooden keeps. The construction geometry is considered masterful — the walls curve outward, impossible to climb.

name: Naoshima Island
access: Ferry from Uno port (Okayama), 20 min
admission: Benesse Museum ¥1,300; Chichu ¥2,100; Art House ¥420 per house
description: Japan's most celebrated contemporary art island — a former industrial wasteland transformed by Tadao Ando and the Benesse Foundation into a world-class open-air museum set among rice paddies and sea breezes.

name: Ritsurin Koen Garden
access: 15 min walk from Takamatsu station
description: One of Japan's finest historical landscape gardens — 100 years of development by Takamatsu domain lords created 6 ponds, 13 hills, and 1,400 manicured pines.

name: Shiwaku Islands
access: Ferry from Marugame, 35 min
description: Former pilot-guild islands where medieval Japan's greatest navigators lived. Honjima Island preserves one of Japan's finest concentrations of traditional merchant houses from the 17th–19th centuries.

name: Shodoshima Island
access: Ferry from Takamatsu, 60 min; car ferry also available
description: Kagawa's largest island — olive groves, Angel Road tidal sandbar, soy sauce breweries and sesame oil production. Often called Japan's Greece for its Mediterranean microclimate.

name: Takamatsu Castle (Tamamo Park)
access: 5 min walk from Takamatsu station
description: One of Japan's three great sea castles — the tide once entered directly into the moat, and on storm days still does. The elegant stone walls rise from the Seto Inland Sea with unusual beauty.

name: Teshima Island
access: Ferry from Takamatsu, 35 min; or from Naoshima, 25 min
description: Naoshima's quieter sister — the Teshima Art Museum is a concrete shell without pillars or artificial light, built over a spring. Water bubbles from the floor and light enters through apertures in the ceiling.

name: Yashima Plateau
access: Yashima cable car, 5 min (or 60 min hike)
description: A flat-topped mountain (292m) rising from the sea like an ancient stage — site of the decisive 1185 Yashima naval battle (Genpei War). The plateau-edge views over the Seto Inland Sea are magnificent and overlooked by tourists.

name: Zentsuji Temple
access: 10 min walk from Zentsuji station (JR Dosan Line)
description: The largest and most sacred temple on the 88-temple pilgrimage — the birthplace of Kobo Daishi (Kukai), founder of Shingon Buddhism, in 774 AD. The underground tunnel walk is uniquely immersive.
